claims.flip_evidence_text: “Material Adverse Effect” means, with respect to any Person, any event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect that (i) has, or would have, a material adverse effect on the business, financial condition or results of operations of such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (ii) would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair the ability of such Person to consummate the Transactions
claims.notes: The MAE definition explicitly uses forward-looking language (“would have,” “would reasonably be expected to”), so the correct answer to whether a forward-looking standard exists is Yes.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means, with respect to any Person, any event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect that (i) has, or would have, a material adverse effect on the business, financial condition or results of operations of such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (ii) would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair the ability of such Person to consummate the Transactions; provided, however, in the case of clause (i) only, no event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect to the extent directly or indirectly resulting from, arising out of, attributable to, or related to any of the following shall be deemed to be or constitute a “Material Adverse Effect” or shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would occur: (A) changes in conditions or developments generally applicable to the oil and gas exploration, development or production industry in the United States or any area or areas where the assets of such Person or any of its Subsidiaries are located, including any increase in operating costs or capital expenses or any reduction in drilling activity or production, or changes in Law or regulation affecting such industry; (B) general economic or political conditions or securities, credit, financial or other capital markets conditions (or changes in such conditions), including changes generally in supply, demand, price levels, interest rates, changes in the price of any commodity (including Hydrocarbons) or general market prices, changes in the cost of fuel, sand or proppants and changes in exchange rates, in each case in the United States or any foreign jurisdiction; (C) any failure, in and of itself, by such Person to meet any internal or published projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions in respect of revenues, earnings, production or other financial or operating metrics for any period (it being understood that the events, changes, circumstances, occurrences or effects giving rise to or contributing to such failure may be deemed to constitute or be taken into account in determining whether there has occurred or would occur a Material Adverse Effect); (D) the execution and delivery of this Agreement; (E) the public announcement of the Transactions, including the impact thereof on the relationships, contractual or otherwise, of such Person or any of its Subsidiaries with employees, labor unions, customers, suppliers or partners; (F) any change, in and of itself, in the market price or trading volume of such Person’s securities (it being understood that the events, changes, circumstances, occurrences or effects giving rise to or contributing to such change may be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Material Adverse Effect); (G) any change in applicable Law, COPAS or GAAP (or authoritative interpretation thereof); (H) geopolitical conditions (or changes in such conditions), the outbreak or escalation of hostilities, any acts of war, sabotage or terrorism, or any escalation or worsening of any such acts of war, sabotage or terrorism; (I) any epidemic, pandemic, disease outbreak (including the COVID-19 virus) or other public health crisis or public health event, or the worsening of any of the 99 foregoing; (J) any disruption in the purchase or transportation of crude oil or natural gas produced or otherwise sold by such Person or its Subsidiaries as a result of any shutdown, interruption or declaration of force majeure by any pipeline operator or other purchaser of such products; (K) natural declines in well performance or reclassification or recalculation of reserves in the ordinary course of business; (L) seasonal reductions in revenues and/or earnings of such Person or any of its Subsidiaries in the ordinary course of their respective businesses; (M) any actions taken or omitted to be taken by a Party at the written direction of the other Parties (for the avoidance of doubt any action by, or omission of, a Party for which such Party sought or requested, and the other Parties provided, consent shall not be deemed to be “at the written direction of” such Party); or (N) compliance with the terms of, or the taking of any action expressly required by, this Agreement (except for any obligation under this Agreement to operate in the ordinary course of business (or similar obligation) pursuant to Section 5.1), except to the extent any such event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect directly or indirectly resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to any of the matters described in clauses (A), (B), (G), (H) and (I), has a disproportionate effect on such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated Persons in the oil and gas exploration, development and production industry in the geographic areas in which such Person and any of its Subsidiaries operate (in which case, such event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would occur solely to the extent it is disproportionate). (Pages 109-110)

Question:
MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)

Llama answer:
No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect.

Canonical claims:
- claim-77123bb50fc4: No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true

- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means, with respect to any Person, any event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect that (i) has, or would have, a material adverse effect on the business, financial condition or results of operations of such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (ii) would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair the ability of such Person to consummate the Transactions; provided, however, in the case of clause (i) only, no event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect to the extent directly or indirectly resulting from, arising out of, attributable to, or related to any of the following shall be deemed to be or constitute a “Material Adverse Effect” or shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would occur: (A) changes in conditions or developments generally applicable to the oil and gas exploration, development or production industry in the United States or any area or areas where the assets of such Person or any of its Subsidiaries are located, including any increase in operating costs or capital expenses or any reduction in drilling activity or production, or changes in Law or regulation affecting such industry; (D) the execution and delivery of this Agreement; (E) the public announcement of the Transactions, including the impact thereof on the relationships, contractual or otherwise, of such Person or any of its Subsidiaries with employees, labor unions, customers, suppliers or partners; (F) any change, in and of itself, in the market price or trading volume of such Person’s securities (it being understood that the events, changes, circumstances, occurrences or effects giving rise to or contributing to such change may be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Material Adverse Effect); (K) natural declines in well performance or reclassification or recalculation of reserves in the ordinary course of business; (L) seasonal reductions in revenues and/or earnings of such Person or any of its Subsidiaries in the ordinary course of their respective businesses; (M) any actions taken or omitted to be taken by a Party at the written direction of the other Parties (for the avoidance of doubt any action by, or omission of, a Party for which such Party sought or requested, and the other Parties provided, consent shall not be deemed to be “at the written direction of” such Party); or (N) compliance with the terms of, or the taking of any action expressly required by, this Agreement (except for any obligation under this Agreement to operate in the ordinary course of business (or similar obligation) pursuant to Section 5.1), except to the extent any such event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect directly or indirectly resulting from, arising out of, attributable to or related to any of the matters described in clauses (A), (B), (G), (H) and (I), has a disproportionate effect on such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, relative to other similarly situated Persons in the oil and gas exploration, development and production industry in the geographic areas in which such Person and any of its Subsidiaries operate (in which case, such event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect (if any) shall be taken into account when determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would occur solely to the extent it is disproportionate). (Pages 109-110)

Question:
MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)

Llama answer:
No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect.

Canonical claims:
- claim-77123bb50fc4: No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate the given claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer.

 Let me break down the components:

 1. **Contract excerpt**: Definition of "Material Adverse Effect" (MAE). Key relevant parts:
    - Clause (C): "any failure, in and of itself, by such Person to meet any internal or published projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions in respect of revenues, earnings, production or other financial or operating metrics for any period (it being understood that the events, changes, circumstances, occurrences or effects giving rise to or contributing to such failure may be deemed to constitute or be taken into account in determining whether there has occurred or would occur a Material Adverse Effect)"
    - Clause (F): "any change, in and of itself, in the market price or trading volume of such Person's securities (it being understood that the events, changes, circumstances, occurrences or effects giving rise to or contributing to such change may be deemed to constitute, or be taken into account in determining whether there has been or will be, a Material Adverse Effect)"
    - The definition includes forward-looking elements like "would have", "would reasonably be expected to prevent", etc.

 2. **Question**: "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)" - asking if the MAE standard includes a forward-looking component/standard.

 3. **Llama answer**: "No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect."

 4. **Canonical claim**: "claim-77123bb50fc4: No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect."

 Wait, I need to be careful here. The question asks "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)". 

 Looking at the contract:
 - The MAE definition explicitly uses forward-looking language: "has, or would have" and "would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair"
 - So the MAE definition DOES include a forward-looking standard. The answer to "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)" should arguably be "Yes" because of the "would have" / "would reasonably be expected to" language.

 However, the Llama answer says "No" and claims it does not include forward-looking statements. 

 But wait - the claim specifically says "forward-looking statements" not "forward-looking standard". Let me read carefully:
 - Llama answer: "No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect."
 - Claim: "No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect."

 What does the contract say about forward-looking statements?
 - Clause (C) explicitly addresses "projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions" and says failure to meet them, in and of itself, is not an MAE. But the underlying causes may be considered.
 - The definition itself uses forward-looking language ("would have", "would reasonably be expected to").

 The claim/answer seems to conflate "forward-looking statements" with a "forward-looking standard". The question asks about "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)". 

 Actually, looking at this more carefully:
 - The contract explicitly includes forward-looking language in the MAE definition ("would have", "would reasonably be expected to"). This means the MAE standard DOES have a forward-looking component/standard.
 - However, the Llama answer says "No" regarding forward-looking statements.

 But the claim is specifically about "forward-looking statements" not being included as a basis. The contract does mention projections/forecasts (which are forward-looking statements) in clause (C), but explicitly says failure to meet them is not, in and of itself, an MAE.

 Wait, I need to assess: Is the claim true or false?

 The question is: "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)"
 The claim/answer says: No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis.

 Actually, I think there might be confusion in the question itself. Let me look at what a "forward-looking standard" means in MAE clauses. In M&A law, an MAE clause can be assessed based on:
 1. Historical effects (what has already happened)
 2. Forward-looking/expected future effects ("would reasonably be expected to" language)

 This contract explicitly includes forward-looking language: "would have" and "would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair". So the answer to "Is there a forward-looking standard?" should be YES.

 Therefore, the Llama answer ("No") appears to be FALSE.

 But wait - I need to look at the exact wording of the claim. The claim says: "No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect."

 Is this claim correct based on the contract?
 - The contract explicitly includes "would reasonably be expected to" which is forward-looking.
 - The contract explicitly mentions "projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions" (which are forward-looking statements) and says failure to meet them is not itself an MAE, but the underlying causes may be.
 - So forward-looking statements (projections, forecasts) ARE referenced in the MAE definition, even if only to exclude them as standalone evidence.

 However, the claim says the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements "as a basis". This is tricky. The MAE standard does include forward-looking language as part of its operative test (clause ii). And forward-looking statements (projections) are explicitly mentioned, though excluded from standalone consideration.

 Actually, I think the key issue is: The question asks whether there is a forward-looking standard. The contract clearly has one ("would have", "would reasonably be expected to"). So the correct answer to the question is "Yes". The Llama answer says "No", which is incorrect.

 Therefore, the claim (which matches the Llama answer) should be labeled FALSE.

 But I need to be very careful. Let me reconsider if there's any interpretation where the claim is true.

 Could "forward-looking statements" refer specifically to clause (C) - projections, forecasts, etc.? The clause says failure to meet them is not itself an MAE. So they are not a basis for determining MAE. But the question asks "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)" which likely refers to whether the MAE test looks to future/prospective effects. 

 Wait, looking at the contract again:
 - "(i) has, or would have, a material adverse effect..." - this includes forward-looking "would have"
 - "(ii) would reasonably be expected to prevent..." - this is entirely forward-looking

 So the standard definitely IS forward-looking in part. The answer should be Yes.

 The Llama answer and claim both say No. This is false.

 Now, is the claim load-bearing? Load-bearing means that if this claim were flipped (i.e., if the evidence contradicted it), it would change the overall answer. Since this is the core answer to the question, yes, it is load-bearing.

 Wait, the instructions say: "load_bearing_label: yes or no" and "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string"

 For load-bearing, I need to provide evidence text that would flip the claim. Since the claim is false, I need to provide the text from the contract that contradicts it and would support the opposite conclusion.

 The flip evidence would be the forward-looking language in the contract: "has, or would have" and/or "would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair".

 Let me check if there's any nuance I'm missing. The claim says "forward-looking statements". In legal/financial contexts, "forward-looking statements" often refers to projections, forecasts, and predictions (like in SEC safe harbor provisions). Clause (C) of the contract explicitly addresses these: "any failure, in and of itself, by such Person to meet any internal or published projections, forecasts, estimates or predictions..." and excludes them. 

 So if the question is interpreted as: "Does the MAE standard include forward-looking statements (like projections/forecasts) as a basis?" then the answer might actually be No, because clause (C) explicitly excludes them. But wait, the question says "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)" not "Does the MAE standard include forward-looking statements?"

 The question "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)" is ambiguous, but typically in contract analysis, this asks whether the MAE definition includes a forward-looking component (i.e., does it ask "would reasonably be expected to" or only "has had"). Since the contract clearly includes "would reasonably be expected to", the answer is Yes.

 But the Llama answer interprets it as asking about forward-looking statements (projections, etc.) and says No. However, even on that interpretation, is it fully correct? The contract does reference forward-looking statements (projections, forecasts) in clause (C), so to say the standard does not include them at all might be partially true (they're included but excluded as a basis), but the answer says "does not include forward-looking statements as a basis", which is arguably true regarding clause (C).

 Hmm, this is genuinely ambiguous. Let me think about how these datasets are usually constructed. Usually:
 - Question: MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N) -> asking if the MAE definition looks to future effects
 - If the contract says "would reasonably be expected to", answer is Y (Yes)
 - If the contract only says "has had", answer is N (No)

 This contract says both "has, or would have" and "would reasonably be expected to". So the answer is definitely Yes.

 The Llama answer says No. This is wrong.

 But the claim is: "No, the MAE standard does not include forward-looking statements as a basis for determining a Material Adverse Effect."

 I think the safest annotation is:
 - correctness_label: false
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: The forward-looking language from the contract: "has, or would have, a material adverse effect" and "would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair"
 - notes: The contract explicitly includes forward-looking language ("would have", "would reasonably be expected to") in the MAE definition.

 Wait, but I need to be careful about the exact wording. The claim mentions "forward-looking statements" which could be different from a "forward-looking standard". A forward-looking standard refers to the predictive test ("would reasonably be expected to"). Forward-looking statements refer to projections, forecasts, etc.

 If the question is "MAE Forward looking standard (Y/N)", and the Llama answer talks about "forward-looking statements", the Llama might be answering a slightly different question. But as a proxy annotator, I must compare the claim against the contract and the question.

 Given the question asks about a "Forward looking standard", and the contract clearly has one, the correct answer is Yes. The claim says No. Thus the claim is false.

 Let me provide:
 - correctness_label: false
 - load_bearing_label: yes  
 - flip_evidence_text: "any event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect that (i) has, or would have, a material adverse effect on the business, financial condition or results of operations of such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (ii) would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair the ability of such Person to consummate the Transactions"
 - notes: Optional short note explaining that the contract contains explicit forward-looking language.

 Actually, looking at the flip_evidence_text, it should be the text that flips the claim - i.e., evidence that there IS a forward-looking standard. That would be the text I quoted.

 Let me double-check: Is the claim partially true? Could one argue that while there is a forward-looking standard, there is no inclusion of forward-looking statements (projections) as a basis? 
 - Clause (C) mentions projections but explicitly excludes them as a standalone basis. So one could argue that forward-looking statements are not a basis.
 - However, the question asks about a "forward looking standard", not "forward looking statements". The claim conflates these or directly answers "No" to the question.
 - Since the question is Y/N and the claim answers "No", and the correct answer is "Yes" (because of the "would have" / "would reasonably be expected to" language), the claim is false.

 I will mark it as false.

 Wait, one more check: The prompt says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." It doesn't explicitly give a "model answer" separate from the Llama answer and Canonical claims. The "Llama answer" is the model answer? Or is there a separate canonical answer? The prompt mentions "Canonical claims" at the end. Actually, looking at the prompt structure:

 - Question
 - Llama answer
 - Canonical claims

 This suggests the Llama answer is what the model produced, and the canonical claim is the claim derived from it that needs to be evaluated.

 So I evaluate claim-77123bb50fc4.

 My final assessment:
 - correctness_label: false
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: "any event, change, circumstance, occurrence or effect that (i) has, or would have, a material adverse effect on the business, financial condition or results of operations of such Person and its Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, or (ii) would reasonably be expected to prevent, materially delay or materially impair the ability of such Person to consummate the Transactions"
 - notes: The contract explicitly includes forward-looking language ("would have," "would reasonably be expected to"), establishing a forward-looking standard for MAE.

 I should format this properly as requested.
